excerpt: PSBT-based workflows let a signer operate without ever connecting to the internet. The unsigned transaction is constructed by an online watch-only wallet, transferred to the signer via removable storage or QR code, signed offline after the user verifies destination and amount on the signer's display, and the signed transaction is transferred back to the online wallet for broadcast.
body:
Source excerpt:
PSBT-based workflows let a signer operate without ever connecting to the internet. The unsigned transaction is constructed by an online watch-only wallet, transferred to the signer via removable storage or QR code, signed offline after the user verifies destination and amount on the signer's display, and the signed transaction is transferred back to the online wallet for broadcast.

---

Cited by leaf "Choosing a hardware signer":
The project doesn't recommend specific signer brands — that's the kind of advice that ages badly and that gets read as endorsement when it shouldn't. What's worth offering is the list of properties to evaluate, which doesn't change much over time, plus where to read independent reviews. The four properties that matter most: open and reproducibly-built firmware, a verifying display under the device's control, no general-purpose execution environment, and a documented track record of how the vendor handled past vulnerability disclosures [1].

Open firmware lets independent reviewers audit the signing logic, the entropy sources, and the PSBT-handling code. Reproducible builds let you verify the binary running on the device matches the published source — without that, "open firmware" is a marketing claim. The verifying display is the load-bearing security feature: the user must be able to see destination address and amount on the signer's screen, and that screen must be driven by the signer itself, not by a host computer that could lie about what it's showing. No general-purpose execution means the device runs only its signing firmware — no app store, no installable plugins, no "smart" features that expand the attack surface [2].

Vulnerability handling is the property people most often skip when comparing signers and the one that probably matters most over a multi-year holding period. Every signer that has been examined seriously by independent researchers has had vulnerabilities disclosed; the question is whether the vendor responded promptly with fixes, credited the researcher, and published an honest post-mortem, or whether they downplayed and stalled. Look for a public security page that lists past CVEs by ID with disclosure dates and resolution dates [3]. For specific brand recommendations, the only honest answer is to read independent reviewers who do this full-time — the project's job is to teach you what to look for, not to rank specific vendors.

---

Cited by leaf "Hardware signers and the air-gap":
A hardware signer is a small device whose only job is to hold private keys and sign transactions you hand it. It never holds funds, never connects to the network, and ideally never speaks to anything except a microSD card or a QR code reader. The threat model it defends against is malware on your everyday computer: a laptop that's been compromised can spy on your screen, swap clipboard addresses, and watch you type, but it cannot sign a transaction without the signer's keys, and the signer's screen shows you the actual address and amount it's about to authorize [1].

"Air-gapped" means the signer has no network interface — no USB data, no Bluetooth, no Wi-Fi. Communication is via QR code or removable storage, with a partially-signed bitcoin transaction (PSBT) carried in and a signed PSBT carried out. The threat-model improvement over USB-connected signers is real but bounded: USB attacks against widely-used signers do exist in the literature, but most successful key-extraction events have been physical (chip decap, voltage glitching), not remote. The bigger practical win is workflow: you sign on a machine that has no general-purpose code execution, so phishing and malicious browser extensions cannot sneak transactions past your verification step [2].

The one structural choice that matters more than vendor selection is whether the signer's firmware and (where applicable) its hardware design are open and reproducible. Open firmware lets independent reviewers audit the signing logic, the entropy sources, and the PSBT-handling code; reproducible builds let you verify the binary on the device matches the published source [3]. This is the relevant axis to evaluate signers on. Concrete vendor advice — "buy this brand, avoid that one" — is exactly the kind of thing the project doesn't go into; for that, read independent reviews from people who do this full-time and pay attention to who has had vulnerabilities disclosed and how they handled them.
